text: The laparoscopic view shows the left ovary with multiple cysts, consistent with the ultrasound findings. The ovary is prepared for removal due to recurrent endometriosis.
The surgeon inspects the ureter, observing its path from the kidney to the bladder. An implant of endometriosis is visible on the right pelvis, near the site where the ovary was previously removed.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic hook to spread the pelvic tissue, while employing Maryland bipolar forceps set at thirty thirty to grasp and cauterize tissue as needed.

The surgeon inspects the right ureter, visible as it courses from the kidney to the bladder. The endometrial implant located at the back of the vagina is ablated using a laparoscopic hook, with other areas of endometriosis also visible.

The surgeon identifies additional areas of endometriosis at the left pelvic brim near the descending colon. Pulsating vascular structures are visible to the left of the endometrial implants, while the descending colon is situated to the right. The surgeon exercises caution with energy use due to the proximity of these structures.
The surgeon inspects and ablates additional endometrial implants. The urocycal ligament suspension of the vagina is visible, showing the archway structure that supports the vaginal suspension.

The surgeon grasps the left ovary and drains the endometrioma, a chocolate cyst, to decrease its size for removal through the umbilicus. The dark, liquid chocolate-like appearance of the cyst is visible as it is drained from the ovary.
The assistant uses a suction irrigator to remove the drained contents of the chocolate cysts, revealing three to four endometriomas in the surgical field.

The surgeon places the decompressed ovary, previously drained of cysts, into an endo pouch. This pouch facilitates the removal of the ovary through the umbilical incision, avoiding the need for a larger surgical cut.

Following the removal of the ovary, the surgeon inspects the interior of the bladder, revealing an endometriosis implant. The implant is visually identified within the bladder, correlating with the patient's symptoms of urinary tract infection and bladder spasms.
